865	\begin{lemma}[Cohomology of the applied resolution as a cosyzygy cokernel]
866	  \label{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution}
867	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso, CategoryTheory.Functor.gHomologyZeroIso}
868	  % NOTE (iter-007 review): this lemma states BOTH the degree-0 case (H⁰ ≅ G(A)) and the
869	  % positive-degree case (Hⁿ ≅ coker). The Lean split is unavoidable — the n=0 and n≥1 targets
870	  % have different types — so the \lean{} names both decls: cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso (n≥1)
871	  % and gHomologyZeroIso (n=0). Both are sorry-free + axiom-clean.
872	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
873	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
874	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA) and Lemma
875	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
876	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838 and L5619--5654).
877	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
878	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
879	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
880	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
881	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
882	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}) and Tag 015D
883	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
884	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
885	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, the cohomology of the applied complex
886	  \(G(J^\bullet) = \bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to \cdots\bigr)\) is computed by
887	  \[
888	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\; G(A),
889	    \qquad
890	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\;
891	      \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr) \quad (n \geq 1),
892	  \]
893	  where \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) is \(G\) applied to the surjection
894	  \(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n\) of \((S_{n-1})\).
895	\end{lemma}
896	
897	\begin{proof}
898	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
899	  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
900	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
901	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
902	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
903	  \[
904	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
905	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
906	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
907	  \]
908	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
909	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
910	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
911	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
912	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
913	  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
914	  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
915	  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
916	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
917	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
918	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
919	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
920	\end{proof}

961	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
962	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.
963	  Let

994	% $$

996	% $$

1001	    lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self, lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
1002	    \leanok
1003	  The argument is the classical dimension shift, now assembled from the cosyzygy
1004	  infrastructure and the dimension-shift step proved above. Cut the resolution into
1005	  its cosyzygy short exact sequences \((S_m) : 0 \to Z^m \to J^m \to Z^{m+1} \to 0\)
1006	  with \(Z^0 = A\) and acyclic middle terms, as furnished by
1007	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}.
1008	
1009	  \emph{Degree zero.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution},
1010	  \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) \cong G(A)\), and by \(R^0 G \cong G\)
1011	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) the right-hand side is \((R^0 G)(A)\).
1012	  This is the case \(n = 0\).
1013	
1014	  \emph{Positive degrees.} Fix \(n \geq 1\). The sequences
1015	  \((S_0), \ldots, (S_{n-2})\) all have acyclic middle terms, so the dimension-shift
1016	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} applied to them gives, for each \(k \geq 1\),
1017	  isomorphisms \((R^k G)(Z^{m+1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(Z^m)\) for \(0 \le m \le n-2\)
1018	  (with \(Z^0 = A\)). Composing them down the staircase
1019	  \[
1020	    (R^n G)(A)
1021	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
1022	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
1023	      \;\cong\; \cdots
1024	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1})
1025	  \]
1026	  reduces \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor. (For \(n = 1\) the
1027	  staircase is empty and \((R^1 G)(A) = (R^1 G)(Z^0)\).) Now apply the lowest-degree
1028	  cokernel description (Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}) to \((S_{n-1})\),
1029	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), whose middle term \(J^{n-1}\) is
1030	  acyclic:
1031	  \[
1032	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
1033	  \]

23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	
26	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
27	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
28	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
29	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
30	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
31	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
32	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
33	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
34	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
35	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
36	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
37	
38	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
39	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
40	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
41	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
42	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
43	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
44	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
45	\[
46	  \begin{array}{ccc}
47	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
48	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
49	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
50	  \end{array}
51	\]
52	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
53	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
54	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
55	
56	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
57	
58	\begin{definition}
59	
60	\leanok
61	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
62	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
63	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
64	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
65	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of

68	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
69	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.

73	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate

76	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
77	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
78	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
79	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
80	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
81	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
82	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
83	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
84	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
85	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
86	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
87	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
88	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
89	  \[
90	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
91	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
92	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
93	  \]
94	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
95	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
96	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
97	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
98	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
99	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
100	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
101	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
102	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
103	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
104	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
105	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
106	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
107	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
108	\end{definition}
109	
110	\begin{definition}
111	
112	\leanok
113	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
114	  \label{def:cech_complex}
115	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
116	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
117	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
118	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
119	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
120	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
121	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
122	  % is identified with the complex
123	  % $$
124	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
125	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
126	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
127	  % \ldots
128	  % $$
129	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
130	  % \begin{equation}
131	  % \label{equation-extended}
132	  % 0 \to
133	  % M \to
134	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
135	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
136	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
137	  % \ldots
138	  % \end{equation}
139	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
140	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
141	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
142	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
143	  vanishing).}
144	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
145	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
146	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
147	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
148	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
149	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
150	  in degree \(p\) it is
151	  \[
152	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
153	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
154	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
155	  \]
156	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
157	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
158	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
159	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
160	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
161	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
162	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
163	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
164	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
165	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
166	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
167	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
168	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
169	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
170	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
171	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
172	\end{definition}
173	
174	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
175	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
176	
177	It is convenient to factor the construction of
178	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
179	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
180	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
181	
182	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
183	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
184	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
185	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
186	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
187	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
188	\[
189	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
190	    \;=\;
191	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
192	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
193	\]
194	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
195	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
196	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
197	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
198	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
199	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
200	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
201	
202	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
203	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
204	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
205	\[
206	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
207	  \qquad
208	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
209	\]
210	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
211	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
212	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
213	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
214	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
215	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
216	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
217	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
218	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
219	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
220	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
221	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
222	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
223	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
224	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
225	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
226	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
227	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
228	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
229	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
230	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
231	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
232	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
233	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
234	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
235	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
236	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
237	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
238	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
239	
240	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
241	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
242	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
243	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
244	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
245	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
246	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
247	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
248	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
249	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
250	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
251	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
252	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
253	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
254	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
255	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
256	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
257	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
258	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
259	
260	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
261	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
262	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
263	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
264	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
265	
266	\begin{definition}\leanok
267	[Arrow of an affine cover]
268	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
269	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
270	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
271	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
272	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
273	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
274	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
275	  apply.
276	\end{definition}
277	
278	\begin{definition}[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
279	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
280	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOver, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOverAug}
281	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
282	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
283	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
284	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
285	  \[
286	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
287	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
288	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
289	  \]
290	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
291	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
292	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
293	\end{definition}
294	
295	\begin{definition}\leanok
296	[Push--pull object map]
297	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
298	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
299	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
300	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
301	  \[
302	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
303	  \]
304	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
305	\end{definition}
306	
307	\begin{definition}[Push--pull comparison map]
308	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_eq_raw, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self_gen, AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardComp_hom_app_id, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_comp}
310	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
311	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
312	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
313	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
314	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
315	  \[
316	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
317	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
318	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
319	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
320	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
321	      \;=\;
322	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
323	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
324	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
325	  \]
326	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
327	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
328	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
329	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
330	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
331	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
332	\end{definition}
333	
334	\begin{lemma}
335	
336	\leanok
337	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
338	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
339	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
340	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
341	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
342	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
343	\end{lemma}
344	
345	\begin{proof}
346	
347	\leanok
348	  A direct calculation with comparison isomorphisms, with no sectionwise content.
349	  Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward comparison as the
350	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison; the identity leg then collapses
351	  by the identity normalisation of the pseudofunctor together with right unitality,
352	  and the two over-triangle transport coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
353	\end{proof}
354	
355	\begin{lemma}
356	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
357	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
358	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_pentagon}
359	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
360	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
361	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
362	  \[
363	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
364	  \]
365	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
366	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
367	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
368	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
369	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
370	\end{lemma}
371	
372	\begin{proof}
373	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
374	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
375	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
376	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
377	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
378	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
379	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
380	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
381	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
382	\end{proof}
383	
384	\begin{lemma}
385	
386	\leanok
387	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
388	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
389	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
390	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
391	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
392	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
393	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
394	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
395	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
396	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
397	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
398	  \[
399	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
400	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
401	    \;=\;
402	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
403	  \]
404	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
405	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
406	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
407	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
408	\end{lemma}
409	
410	\begin{proof}
411	
412	\leanok
413	  A direct mate-calculus computation: unfold both sides using the definition of
414	  the adjunction unit for the composite and the pseudofunctor structure maps, then
415	  verify the equality by the mate identity for the pushforward/pullback comparison.
416	\end{proof}
417	
418	\begin{lemma}
419	
420	\leanok
421	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
422	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
423	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
424	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
425	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the
426	  over-triangle \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\), and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
427	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
428	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
429	  collapses to the transport-light form
430	  \[
431	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
432	      \;\circ\; e_2
433	    \;=\;
434	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
435	      \;\circ\; e_3,
436	  \]
437	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by
438	  \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\). It states the over-triangle cancellation in the
439	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
440	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
441	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
442	  composition (pentagon) law.
443	\end{lemma}
444	
445	\begin{proof}
446	
447	\leanok
448	  Substitute the over-triangle equality \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\) to replace
449	  transport coercions by identities, then verify the cancellation by direct
450	  calculation with the pushforward comparison.
451	\end{proof}
452	
453	\begin{definition}
454	
455	\leanok
456	[Push--pull functor]
457	  \label{def:push_pull_functor}
458	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullFunctor}
459	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
460	  The \emph{push--pull functor}
461	  \[
462	    G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
463	  \]
464	  assembling the object map \(p \mapsto p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F}\) of
465	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_obj} and the comparison morphism map of
466	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a genuine functor. Functoriality is exactly the two
467	  laws established above: the identity law
468	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} and the composition law Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_comp}. The
469	  functor is contravariant in the over-category \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\); it is the lift of the
470	  geometric backbone (1) to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described as step (2) above.
471	\end{definition}
472	
473	\begin{definition}
474	
475	\leanok
476	[{\v C}ech nerve cosimplicial object]
477	  \label{def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial}
478	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechNerveCosimplicial}
479	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor}
480	  The (non-augmented) cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module obtained by transporting the
481	  geometric {\v C}ech backbone in \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\) of

486	  underlying cosimplicial object that the {\v C}ech nerve of
487	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} augments by the structure map \(\mathcal{F} \to

507	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
508	
509	\begin{definition}
510	[Standard affine cover from a spanning family]
511	  \label{def:standard_affine_cover}
512	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}
513	  \mathlibok
514	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Cover.Open}).}
515	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring (so \(R = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R,
516	  \mathcal{O})\)) and let \(s : \iota \to R\) be a family of elements that generate
517	  the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\). The
518	  \emph{standard affine open cover} of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) associated with
519	  \(s\) is the affine open cover whose index set is \(\iota\), whose \(i\)-th member
520	  is the basic open \(D(s_i)\), and whose \(i\)-th structure morphism is the map of
521	  affine schemes induced by the localisation homomorphism \(R \to R_{s_i}\); that
522	  is, in Mathlib notation the defining equation
523	  \(\bigl(\text{affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}\bigr)_f\, i =
524	  \operatorname{Spec.map}\bigl(\text{algebraMap}\; R\; (\operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i))\bigr)\),
525	  so that the sections over the \(i\)-th piece are the \emph{away} localisation
526	  \(R_{s_i} = \operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i)\) and the geometric piece is
527	  \(D(s_i)\). This is exactly the standard open covering
528	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_i D(s_i)\) by basic opens, packaged as a single
529	  cover object. It is the spanning-family bundle \((s, hs)\) that the affine
530	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is stated over.
531	\end{definition}
532	
533	\subsection{The categorical--module bridge (L1)}
534	\label{sec:cech_l1_bridge}
535	
536	The affine vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is a statement about the
537	\emph{section} {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
538	\mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- a cochain complex of
539	abelian groups --- whereas the combinatorial contracting-homotopy core operates on a
540	concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules. The passage between the two, the
541	\emph{categorical--module bridge}, decomposes into two self-contained statements: the
542	section identification (each section group is an away localisation, with the
543	restriction maps the canonical localisation maps) and the homology-to-exactness
544	reduction (vanishing of the abstract homology is equivalent to exactness of the
545	underlying localised-module complex). These are isolated as
546	Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and
547	Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} below; the assembly --- feeding each
548	positive-degree node through the local-to-global exactness criterion and closing with
549	the dependent combinatorial port --- is carried out in the proof of
550	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.
551	
552	\begin{definition}
553	
554	[Quasi-coherent sections over a basic open are an away localisation]
555	  \label{def:qcoh_sections_localized}
556	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.qcohSectionsAwayLocalized,
557	    AlgebraicGeometry.basicOpen_sprod,
558	    AlgebraicGeometry.iInf_fin_succ,
559	    AlgebraicGeometry.qcohRestriction_eq_comparison,
560	    AlgebraicGeometry.tP,
561	    AlgebraicGeometry.tU,
562	    AlgebraicGeometry.phiL,
563	    AlgebraicGeometry.phi,
564	    AlgebraicGeometry.phi_eq_phiL,
565	    AlgebraicGeometry.restr_bridge}
566	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:standard_affine_cover}
567	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
568	  % items (4)--(5) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L701--707).
569	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(4) For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
570	  % as an $R_f$-module.
571	  % (5) Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
572	  % on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
573	  % are the maps
574	  % $R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
575	  % \ref{lemma-standard-open}."
576	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
577	  items (4)--(5).}
578	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
579	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\)-module, and write
580	  \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\) for its module of global
581	  sections. For every \(g \in R\), the restriction-of-sections map
582	  \(M = \mathcal{F}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g))\) exhibits the
583	  section group \(\mathcal{F}(D(g))\) as the away localisation \(M_g\) of \(M\); that
584	  is, this map is an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for the multiplicative
585	  submonoid \(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g\) of powers of \(g\). Moreover, for
586	  \(D(g_2) \subseteq D(g_1)\) the restriction map
587	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(g_1)) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g_2))\) is, under these identifications, the
588	  canonical localisation map \(M_{g_1} \to M_{g_2}\) (the unique \(R\)-linear map
589	  compatible with the localisation units). Item~(4) of Tag~01HV gives the first
590	  assertion for \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) (\(\Gamma(D(g), \widetilde{M}) = M_g\)
591	  as an \(R_g\)-module) and item~(5) gives the second (the restriction along
592	  \(D(g) \subset D(f)\) is the localisation map \(M_f \to M_g\)).
593	
594	  The module-level input on the side of Mathlib is the development
595	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}: the comparison map
596	  \((\operatorname{tilde.toOpen} M\, (D(g))).\mathrm{hom}\) carries the instance
597	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g)\), and
598	  \(\operatorname{isUnit\_algebraMap\_end\_basicOpen}\) records that \(g\) acts
599	  invertibly on the sections over \(D(g)\); the restriction compatibility is
600	  \(\operatorname{tilde.toOpen\_res}\). These supply the statement \emph{verbatim} for
601	  the standard sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\). For an \emph{arbitrary} quasi-coherent
602	  \(\mathcal{F}\) the remaining step is the canonical comparison
603	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})}\), i.e.\
604	  that the counit \(\operatorname{Scheme.Modules.fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism. This
605	  is \emph{not} yet available in Mathlib for a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\):
606	  Mathlib provides it only from a \emph{global} presentation
607	  (\(\operatorname{isIso\_fromTildeΓ\_of\_presentation}\)), whereas quasi-coherence
608	  supplies only \emph{local} presentation data on a cover. Globalising the local data
609	  on the affine \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) --- the affine equivalence
610	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \simeq \operatorname{Mod}(R)\), Stacks
611	  Tag~01I8 --- is the one genuine gap this definition records as a build target; it is
612	  the canonical missing affine equivalence and is self-contained.
613	
614	  Two combinatorial--geometric ingredients package the identification into the form
615	  consumed by Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}. First, the multi-index
616	  intersection identity
617	  \(\bigl(\bigcap_k D(s_{\sigma(k)})\bigr) = D\bigl(\prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\bigr)\) in
618	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R).\mathrm{Opens}\) (\(\operatorname{basicOpen\_sprod}\), proved
619	  by induction on the index length using the infimum-splitting helper
620	  \(\operatorname{iInf\_fin\_succ}\) and \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen\_mul}\)):
621	  it rewrites the \((p+1)\)-fold intersection over a multi-index \(\sigma\) as the single
622	  basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\), so the
623	  section group there is the away localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). Second, the restriction
624	  compatibility of item~(5) is made explicit: for a basic-open inclusion
625	  \(D(b) \subseteq D(a)\) the presheaf restriction map of \(\widetilde{M}\) between the
626	  two section groups \emph{is} the away-localisation comparison
627	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison}\) of the two localisation units
628	  (\(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\), by the uniqueness
629	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison\_unique}\) of a map of localised modules);
630	  this is the single differential brick that the {\v C}ech coface match of
631	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} feeds on.
632	
633	  The identification is packaged by a few helpers: the two localised-module endpoints
634	  \(\operatorname{tP}, \operatorname{tU}\) (the section groups
635	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) named as away localisations at the two indices
636	  involved in a face), the per-\(\sigma\) comparison
637	  \(\varphi_\sigma = \operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.iso}\) realised both as the bare
638	  localisation comparison \(\operatorname{phiL}\) and as its sheaf-level avatar
639	  \(\operatorname{phi}\) (their agreement is \(\operatorname{phi\_eq\_phiL}\)), and the
640	  accessor-bridge \(\operatorname{restr\_bridge}\) that exhibits the presheaf
641	  restriction map through these comparisons.
642	\end{definition}
643	
644	\begin{lemma}
645	[Section {\v C}ech homology vanishes iff the localised complex is exact]
646	  \label{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}
647	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCech_homology_exact,
648	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts,
649	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.isUnit_powers,
650	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.mul,
651	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.of_dvd,
652	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison,
653	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_apply,
654	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp,
655	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp_apply,
656	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp_structure,
657	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_self,
658	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_unique,
659	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechCoeff,
660	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechCoeff_transport_eq_comparison,
661	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechCoface,
662	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechLocalized_exact,
663	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechPrepend,
664	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cech_hcomm,
665	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cech_hsh,
666	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cech_hu,
667	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.sprod,
668	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.sprod_cons,
669	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.sprod_succAbove_dvd}
670	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized, lem:section_cech_module_exact,
671	    lem:section_cech_product_equiv, lem:section_cech_objd_apply,
672	    lem:section_cech_coface_match, lem:section_cech_ab_exact}
673	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
674	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (the {\v C}ech complex is
675	  % the complex of localisations) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
676	  % L58--67).
677	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
678	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
679	  % is identified with the complex
680	  % $$
681	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
682	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
683	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
684	  % \ldots
685	  % $$"
686	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
687	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (identification with the
688	  complex of localisations).}
689	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}
690	  R}\)-module and \(\mathcal{U}\) the standard cover associated with a spanning family
691	  \(s : \iota \to R\). Under the section identification of
692	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}, the section {\v C}ech complex
693	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
694	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} is isomorphic, as a cochain complex, to
695	  the concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules
696	  \[
697	    D^\bullet : \quad
698	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
699	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0,1\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
700	    \cdots,
701	    \qquad
702	    s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)},
703	  \]
704	  whose differential is the alternating sum of the canonical localisation maps. The
705	  intended top-level conclusion is the directed homology vanishing
706	  \[
707	    1 \leq p
708	    \;\Longrightarrow\;
709	    \operatorname{IsZero}\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
710	      \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p\bigr),
711	  \]
712	  which is the form the affine vanishing
713	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} (\(\operatorname{sectionCech\_affine\_vanishing}\))
714	  consumes; it is read off from positive-degree exactness of the underlying group
715	  homomorphisms,
716	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\bigl(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to D^{p+1}\bigr)\), which is
717	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}.
718	
719	  The reduction from the abstract homology to this group-level exactness is \emph{not}
720	  a \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\) statement: the degree-\(p\) object of
721	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) is the \emph{categorical
722	  product}
723	  \(\prod^{c}_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota}
724	  \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) in the category \(\mathrm{Ab}\) of abelian groups (the
725	  alternating-coface-map complex of the \(\mathrm{Ab}\)-valued cosimplicial object
726	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\)), not an \(R\)-module object, so the
727	  module-category criterion \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.moduleCat\_exact\_iff}\) does
728	  not apply. The correct chain runs through three steps, isolated as the sub-lemmas
729	  below: \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology is \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) at
730	  \(p\) (\(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\)); \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\)
731	  of a short complex in \(\mathrm{Ab}\) is the underlying-group exactness
732	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\) via the abelian
733	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
734	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}); and the categorical product is moved to the
735	  per-\(\sigma\) localised modules by the element-level product equivalence
736	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
737	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\)
738	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}), under which the {\v C}ech coface
739	  differential matches the localisation differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of
740	  \(D^\bullet\) (the abstract cosimplicial unfold
741	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply} followed by the tilde-bridge identification
742	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). Transporting the
743	  already-proved exactness \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of
744	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact} across the product equivalence then
745	  discharges the group-level exactness. The active target is the tilde case
746	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\), for which the per-coordinate comparisons are
747	  immediate from \(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\); a general
748	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) is reduced to this case via the affine equivalence
749	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma\mathcal{F}}\) (Stacks Tag~01I8), the
750	  separately-deferred globalisation gap recorded in
751	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}.
752	\end{lemma}
753	
754	\begin{proof}
755	  \uses{def:qcoh_sections_localized, def:section_cech_complex}
756	  The term identification is the degreewise statement of
757	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: the degree-\(p\) section group
758	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is \(\prod_\sigma M_{s_\sigma}\), since
759	  each \((p+1)\)-fold intersection \(D(s_{\sigma(0)}) \cap \cdots \cap
760	  D(s_{\sigma(p)})\) is the basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
761	  \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\) and the sections there are the away
762	  localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). The differential identification is item~(5) of
763	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: each face restriction
764	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})) \to \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is the
765	  canonical localisation map \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\), so the
766	  alternating sum of restrictions is the alternating sum of localisation maps, the
767	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) (the coface match of
768	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). The homology vanishing then follows in
769	  three steps. First, \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\) turns
770	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology at \(p\) into \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) of
771	  the short complex at \(p\). Second, because the degree-\(p\) objects are
772	  \emph{categorical products in \(\mathrm{Ab}\)} rather than \(R\)-modules, the abelian
773	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
774	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}) --- not the module-category criterion ---
775	  reduces \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) to the underlying-group exactness
776	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\). Third, the
777	  element-level product equivalence
778	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
779	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
780	  identifies each product group with the per-\(\sigma\) localised module \(D^p\), so the
781	  group-level exactness is exactly \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to
782	  D^{p+1})\), supplied by \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\)
783	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}).
784	
785	  The identification of the abstract section complex with the concrete localised
786	  complex \(D^\bullet\) --- together with the construction of the prepend transport
787	  \(c\) and the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\) discharging the unit, shift, and
788	  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\)/\(\mathtt{hsh}\)/\(\mathtt{hcomm}\)
789	  of the dependent combinatorial port --- is the bulk of the bridge. The non-circular
790	  route is reflection along the localisation equivalence on its essential image (the
791	  global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is the inverse equivalence there, so it reflects
792	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of homology); it is \emph{not} the assertion that
793	  ``\(\Gamma\) preserves homology'', which would presuppose the very affine exactness
794	  being proved.
795	\end{proof}

797	\begin{lemma}
798	
799	[Element-level product equivalence for the section {\v C}ech terms]
800	  \label{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
801	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechProductEquiv,
802	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechProductEquiv_apply,
803	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionProdAddEquiv}
804	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized}
805	  Let \(\{F_\sigma\}_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota}\) be the finite family of
806	  abelian groups \(F_\sigma = \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) appearing in degree \(p\) of
807	  the section {\v C}ech complex of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex}. There is
808	  an equivalence of underlying types
809	  \[
810	    \operatorname{ToType}\Bigl(\prod^{c}_{\sigma} F_\sigma\Bigr)
811	    \;\simeq\;
812	    \prod_{\sigma} \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma),
813	  \]
814	  between the elements of the categorical product \(\prod^{c}_{\sigma} F_\sigma\) in
815	  \(\mathrm{Ab}\) and the dependent functions \(\sigma \mapsto\) (element of
816	  \(F_\sigma\)). It is supplied by the Mathlib concrete-limit comparison
817	  \(\operatorname{CategoryTheory.Limits.Concrete.productEquiv}\), which holds because the
818	  forgetful functor \(\operatorname{forget}\,\mathrm{Ab}\) preserves the discrete
819	  product (\(\operatorname{PreservesLimit}(\operatorname{Discrete.functor} F)\)). Under
820	  this equivalence each coordinate
821	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma)))\) is identified with the away
822	  localisation \(M_{s_\sigma} = \operatorname{dCoeff}\) of
823	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} by the
824	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) uniqueness of
825	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} (both
826	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) and \(M_{s_\sigma}\) localise \(M\) at \(s_\sigma\), so
827	  the comparison is the canonical isomorphism). This is the move between the
828	  categorical product of \(\mathrm{Ab}\) and the per-\(\sigma\) localised modules.
829	  Its packaging as an additive isomorphism --- the degreewise vertical map of the
830	  ladder transport in Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact} --- is recorded as
831	  \(\operatorname{sectionProdAddEquiv}\), the \(\operatorname{AddEquiv}\) underlying
832	  the coordinatewise comparison.
833	  The coordinate projection of the equivalence is recorded separately as the
834	  definitional restatement
835	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv\_apply}\): the \(\sigma\)-component of the
836	  image of \(y\) under the equivalence is the underlying group map of the categorical
837	  projection \(\pi_\sigma\) applied to \(y\).
838	\end{lemma}
839	
840	\begin{proof}
841	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized}
842	  The type-level equivalence is \(\operatorname{Concrete.productEquiv}\) applied to the
843	  family \(\sigma \mapsto F_\sigma\); its hypothesis is that
844	  \(\operatorname{forget}\,\mathrm{Ab}\) preserves the discrete-diagram limit, which is
845	  the standard instance for \(\mathrm{Ab}\). The per-coordinate identification
846	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))) \cong M_{s_\sigma}\) is the

1523	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1524	  \]
1525	  each factor being the value of the ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1526	  (the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)), with
1527	  differential the alternating sum of restriction maps
1528	  \[
1529	    d(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1530	      = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
1531	        s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}
1532	        \big|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}.
1533	  \]
1534	  This object is \emph{distinct} from the relative {\v C}ech complex
1535	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1536	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}: the latter is the pushforward complex with terms
1537	  \(\prod (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\) in
1538	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\), an object of relative sheaves over the base, whereas the
1539	  section {\v C}ech complex here is the simpler complex of \emph{global sections} of
1540	  \(\mathcal{F}\) over the intersections, an object of abelian groups (the underlying
1541	  additive structure of the sections). The two must not be conflated: the
1542	  presheaf-level acyclicity argument below runs entirely on this section complex.
1543	\end{definition}

1545	\begin{lemma}
1546	[The free complex represents the {\v C}ech complex]

1567	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into}
1568	  % (statement + proof) (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex,
1569	  % L1163--1196).
1570	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Then there is an isomorphism
1571	  % $$
1572	  % \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet, \mathcal{F})
1573	  % =
1574	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})

1577	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
1578	  \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into}.}
1579	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
1580	  open covering. For every presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\)
1581	  there is an isomorphism of cochain complexes of \emph{abelian groups}
1582	  \[
1583	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}\bigl(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1584	      \;=\;
1585	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}),
1586	  \]
1587	  natural in \(\mathcal{F} \in \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\). Here the left side is
1588	  the cochain complex obtained by applying the contravariant
1589	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{F})\) to the chain complex
1590	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
1591	  and the right side is the section {\v C}ech complex of
1592	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex}.
1593	\end{lemma}
1594	
1595	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We saw in the discussion just above the lemma that
1596	% $$
1597	% \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(
1598	% (j_{i_0\ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots i_p}},
1599	% \mathcal{F})
1600	% =
1601	% \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0\ldots i_p}).
1602	% $$
1603	% Hence we see that it is indeed the case that the direct sum
1604	% $$
1605	% \bigoplus\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
1606	% (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}
1607	% $$
1608	% represents the functor
1609	% $$
1610	% \mathcal{F}
1611	% \longmapsto
1612	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0\ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0\ldots i_p}).
1613	% $$
1614	% Hence by Categories, Yoneda Lemma \ref{categories-lemma-yoneda}
1615	% we see that there is a complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$ with terms
1616	% as given. It is a simple matter to see that the maps are as given
1617	% in the lemma."
1618	\begin{proof}
1619	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:section_cech_complex}
1620	  The free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules gives, composed with the
1621	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, \mathcal{F}) \cong
1622	  \mathcal{F}(V)\), the natural identification, for each multi-index,
1623	  \[
1624	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}\bigl(
1625	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),\, \mathcal{F}
1626	    \bigr)
1627	    \;\cong\;
1628	    \operatorname{Hom}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p},\,
1629	      \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1630	    \;\cong\;
1631	    \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),
1632	  \]
1633	  where the last group is the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open
1634	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). Taking the product over multi-indices,
1635	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_p, \mathcal{F}) =
1636	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) =
1637	  \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\); the direct sum
1638	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) therefore represents the functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto
1639	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\). Under this
1640	  identification the \((-1)^j\) maps \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0
1641	  \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j}
1642	  \ldots i_{p+1}})\) defining the differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) become
1643	  exactly the alternating-sum restriction differential of
1644	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so the two cochain
1645	  complexes coincide; naturality in \(\mathcal{F}\) is immediate from the
1646	  naturality of the free–forgetful adjunction and of Yoneda. The per-multi-index
1647	  bijection \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,
1648	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}), \mathcal{F}) \cong \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\) is additive,
1649	  so it is promoted to an isomorphism of abelian groups (an \emph{additive}
1650	  equivalence); taking the product over multi-indices and assembling these degreewise
1651	  additive isomorphisms — which commute with the alternating-sum differentials — yields
1652	  the complex isomorphism by matching components degree by degree.
1653	\end{proof}

1685	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}.}
1686	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1687	  covering. The \emph{cover structure presheaf} \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} :
1688	  X.\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}\) is the image presheaf of the augmentation
1689	  \[
1690	    \coprod_i \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_i) \;\longrightarrow\; \mathbf{1},
1691	  \]
1692	  where the target \(\mathbf{1} = \operatorname{PresheafOfModules.unit}(
1693	  X.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf}.\mathrm{obj})\) is the structure presheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\)
1694	  regarded as a presheaf of modules over itself. Concretely
1695	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when \(W\) is contained in some
1696	  \(U_i\), and \(0\) otherwise. The degree-\(0\) term
1697	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_0 = \coprod_i \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_i)\) of the free
1698	  presheaf complex of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} carries the
1699	  augmentation map \(K(\mathcal{U})_0 \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\) onto this image
1700	  presheaf.
1701	
1702	  The parallel \texttt{...Fam} declarations pinned above are the cover-agnostic
1703	  raw-finite-family mirror of this construction (an arbitrary finite family of opens,
1704	  no covering hypothesis), feeding the family form of the {\v C}ech acyclicity lemma.
1705	\end{definition}

1707	\begin{lemma}
1708	[Objectwise detection of quasi-isomorphisms of presheaf-of-module complexes]
1709	  \label{lem:quasiIso_of_evaluation}
1710	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.quasiIso_of_evaluation,
1711	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_Fmap_homologyMap,
1712	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_of_evaluation}
1713	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1714	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1715	  % (proof, the sectionwise-reduction step) (read from
1716	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1220--1226).
1717	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "It suffices to show that taking sections of this extended
1718	  % complex over any open $W \subset X$ leads to an acyclic complex."
1719	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1720	  (sectionwise reduction).}
1721	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of rings on a category \(C\) and let
1722	  \(\varphi : K \to L\) be a morphism of chain complexes in the abelian category
1723	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. Suppose that for \emph{every}
1724	  object \(V\) the evaluated morphism — the image of \(\varphi\) under the functor
1725	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\bigr).
1726	  \operatorname{mapHomologicalComplex}\), a morphism of complexes of
1727	  \(R(V)\)-modules — is a quasi-isomorphism. Then \(\varphi\) is a
1728	  quasi-isomorphism. This is the objectwise-reduction step that turns the
1729	  resolution claim of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} into a family of
1730	  sectionwise (combinatorial) statements: homology in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) is
1731	  computed open-by-open, because the evaluation functors
1732	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) are exact (hence preserve
1733	  homology) and jointly conservative: a morphism in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) is an isomorphism
1734	  iff each of its evaluations is an isomorphism, and each evaluation functor preserves
1735	  homology.
1736	\end{lemma}
1737	
1738	\begin{proof}
1739	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1740	  A morphism of complexes is a quasi-isomorphism iff it induces an isomorphism on
1741	  homology in every degree (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_iff}\),
1742	  \(\operatorname{quasiIsoAt\_iff\_isIso\_homologyMap}\)). Fix a degree \(i\). The
1743	  homology map \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) iff each of
1744	  its evaluations is an isomorphism, since the underlying
1745	  presheaf-of-abelian-groups functor reflects isomorphisms and a morphism of
1746	  presheaves is an isomorphism iff it is so objectwise (joint conservativity). For
1747	  each \(V\), evaluation preserves homology, so the evaluation of \(H_i(\varphi)\)
1748	  is the homology map of the evaluated complex morphism; the latter is an
1749	  isomorphism because the evaluated morphism is a quasi-isomorphism by hypothesis.
1750	  Hence \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), i.e.\ \(\varphi\) is a
1751	  quasi-isomorphism.
1752	\end{proof}

1754	\begin{lemma}
1755	
1756	[Sectionwise description of the evaluated free complex]
1757	  \label{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}
1758	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeEval_X,
1759	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYonedaEval_iso_of_le,
1760	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYonedaEval_isZero_of_not_le,
1761	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeEval_XFam}
1762	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:cover_structure_presheaf}
1763	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1764	  % (proof, the sectionwise description of $K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$) (read from
1765	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1227--1252).
1766	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Write $I = I_1 \amalg I_2$ where $W \subset U_i$ if and only
1767	  % if $i \in I_1$. ... If $I_1 \not = \emptyset$, then
1768	  % $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)$ and
1769	  % $K^{ext}_p(W) = \bigoplus\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p \in I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(W)$.
1770	  % This is true because of the simple description of the presheaves
1771	  % $(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}$."
1772	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1773	  (description of the evaluated complex).}
1774	  Fix an open \(V \subset X\) and write \(I_1 = I_1(V) = \{\,i \in I : V \leq U_i\,\}\)
1775	  for the set of cover members containing \(V\). Evaluating the free presheaf complex
1776	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} at
1777	  \(V\) gives, degreewise,
1778	  \[
1779	    K(\mathcal{U})_p(V)
1780	      = \bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I}
1781	        \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma)(V)
1782	      = \bigoplus_{\substack{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I \\ V \leq U_\sigma}}
1783	        \mathcal{O}_X(V)
1784	      = \bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V),
1785	  \]
1786	  because \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,W)(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) when
1787	  \(V \leq W\) and \(0\) otherwise (poset hom-set is a subsingleton), and
1788	  \(V \leq U_\sigma = U_{\sigma(0)} \cap \cdots \cap U_{\sigma(p)}\) holds iff
1789	  \(\sigma\) takes all its values in \(I_1\). Under this identification the
1790	  differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet(V)\) is the alternating-sum combinatorial
1791	  {\v C}ech differential of the full simplex on the index set \(I_1\) with
1792	  coefficients in the single module \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\). Moreover the evaluated
1793	  augmentation target is \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) when
1794	  \(I_1 \neq \varnothing\) and \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = 0\) when
1795	  \(I_1 = \varnothing\). Thus the evaluation at \(V\) of the augmented complex
1796	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}[0]\)
1797	  is precisely the augmented combinatorial
1798	  {\v C}ech complex of the simplex on \(I_1\) with coefficients \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\).
1799	  % NOTE: Lean assembly of the degreewise iso: (i) evaluation commutes with the
1800	  % coproduct (cechFreeEval_X); (ii) a surviving summand (V \leq U_\sigma) evaluates
1801	  % to \mathcal{O}_X(V) (freeYonedaEval_iso_of_le); (iii) a non-surviving summand
1802	  % is zero (freeYonedaEval_isZero_of_not_le). The cover-agnostic family mirror is
1803	  % cechFreeEval_XFam, used by the family form of the Cech acyclicity lemma.
1804	\end{lemma}
1805	
1806	\begin{proof}
1807	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:cover_structure_presheaf}
1808	  The degreewise identity is the value at \(V\) of the explicit description of the
1809	  free presheaf \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,W)\) recorded in
1810	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}: it sends \(V\) to the free
1811	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-module on \(\operatorname{Hom}(V, W)\), which is
1812	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) if \(V \leq W\) and \(0\) otherwise. Applying
1813	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation}\;V\) to the coproduct decomposition
1814	  \(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex\_X}\) commutes with the direct sum and
1815	  collapses each summand to \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) or \(0\) according to whether
1816	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\), i.e.\ whether \(\sigma\) factors through \(I_1\); the surviving
1817	  summands are exactly those indexed by \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\). The
1818	  evaluated faces are the index-dropping restriction maps, which on the surviving
1819	  summands are identities of \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\), so the alternating sum is the
1820	  combinatorial {\v C}ech differential. The augmentation value follows from
1821	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) exactly when some \(U_i\)
1822	  contains \(V\) (\(I_1 \neq \varnothing\)), recorded in
1823	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_structure_presheaf}.
1824	\end{proof}

1846	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Moreover, the differential of the complex $K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$
1847	  % is given by
1848	  % $$
1849	  % d(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} =
1850	  % \sum\nolimits_{j = 0, \ldots, p + 1} \sum\nolimits_{i \in I_1}
1851	  % (-1)^j s_{i_0 \ldots i_{j - 1} i i_j \ldots i_p}.
1852	  % $$
1853	  % The sum is finite as the element $s$ has finite support.
1854	  % Fix an element $i_{\text{fix}} \in I_1$. Define a map
1855	  % $$
1856	  % h : K^{ext}_p(W) \longrightarrow K^{ext}_{p + 1}(W)
1857	  % $$
1858	  % by the rule
1859	  % $h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}} =
1860	  % \left\{ \begin{matrix}
1861	  % 0 & \text{if} & i_0 \not = i_{\text{fix}} \\
1862	  % s_{i_1 \ldots i_{p + 1}} & \text{if} & i_0 = i_{\text{fix}}
1863	  % \end{matrix} \right.$"
1864	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1865	  (differential and contracting homotopy of the evaluated complex).}
1866	  Fix a nonempty index set \(J\) (in the application \(J = I_1(V)\)), a chosen index
1867	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} \in J\), and a single coefficient module \(M\). The
1868	  \emph{constant-coefficient combinatorial {\v C}ech engine} consists of: the
1869	  alternating-sum {\v C}ech differential \(\operatorname{combDifferential}\) on the
1870	  full simplex over \(J\) with coefficients \(M\),
1871	  \[
1872	    (\operatorname{combDifferential} t)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1873	      = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\, t_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}},
1874	  \]
1875	  which squares to zero, \(\operatorname{combDifferential} \circ
1876	  \operatorname{combDifferential} = 0\) (\(\operatorname{combDifferential\_comp}\)); the
1877	  \emph{prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\)} homotopy \(\operatorname{combHomotopy}\),
1878	  \((\operatorname{combHomotopy}\, i_{\mathrm{fix}}\, u)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1879	  = u_{i_1 \ldots i_{p+1}}\) when \(i_0 = i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) and \(0\) otherwise; and
1880	  the contracting identity \(d \circ h + h \circ d = \mathrm{id}\)

8029	\begin{lemma}
8030	[Push--pull on a binary coproduct of two legs is the product of the two leg push--pulls]
8031	  \label{lem:pushPull_binary_coprod_prod}
8032	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_binary_coprod_prod,
8033	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_prodLift_of_isLimit,
8034	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_map_prodLift_of_isLimit,
8035	    AlgebraicGeometry.coprodDecompMap,
8036	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_coprodDecompMap}
8037	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, lem:pushPull_binary_leg_coherence, lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf,
8038	    lem:coprodPresheafObjIso_mathlib, lem:isProductOfDisjoint_mathlib,
8039	    lem:evaluation_preserves_products_mathlib}
8040	  Let \(Y_0, Y_1 : \operatorname{Over} X\) be two objects of the slice category over a scheme \(X\),
8041	  and form the binary coproduct of their underlying schemes \(Y_0.\mathrm{left} \amalg Y_1.\mathrm{left}\)
8042	  with structure map
8043	  \(\operatorname{coprod.desc}\,Y_0.\mathrm{hom}\,Y_1.\mathrm{hom} : Y_0.\mathrm{left} \amalg
8044	  Y_1.\mathrm{left} \to X\). Then the push--pull object on this binary coproduct is the binary product
8045	  of the two per-leg push--pull objects:
8046	  \[
8047	    \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,\Bigl(\operatorname{Over.mk}\bigl(
8048	      \operatorname{coprod.desc}\,Y_0.\mathrm{hom}\,Y_1.\mathrm{hom}\bigr)\Bigr)
8049	      \;\cong\;
8050	    \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_0 \;\times\; \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_1
8051	    \qquad \text{in } X.\mathrm{Modules}.
8052	  \]
8053	\end{lemma}
8054	\begin{proof}
8055	  \uses{lem:pushPull_binary_leg_coherence, lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf,
8056	    lem:coprodPresheafObjIso_mathlib, lem:isProductOfDisjoint_mathlib,
8057	    lem:evaluation_preserves_products_mathlib}
8058	  Write \(q = \operatorname{coprod.desc}\,Y_0.\mathrm{hom}\,Y_1.\mathrm{hom}\) and \(M =
8059	  q^*\mathcal{F}\); let \(\operatorname{inl} : Y_0.\mathrm{left} \to Y_0.\mathrm{left} \amalg
8060	  Y_1.\mathrm{left}\) and \(\operatorname{inr} : Y_1.\mathrm{left} \to Y_0.\mathrm{left} \amalg
8061	  Y_1.\mathrm{left}\) be the coproduct inclusions, each an arrow over \(X\) (since \(q \circ
8062	  \operatorname{inl} = Y_0.\mathrm{hom}\) and \(q \circ \operatorname{inr} = Y_1.\mathrm{hom}\)), and
8063	  write \(\overline{\operatorname{inl}}, \overline{\operatorname{inr}}\) for these over-arrows.
8064	
8065	  \emph{The canonical comparison (mandatory framing).} The asserted isomorphism is taken to be the
8066	  \emph{canonical} map
8067	  \[
8068	    \operatorname{prod.lift}\bigl(\operatorname{pushPullMap}\mathcal{F}\,\overline{\operatorname{inl}},\,
8069	      \operatorname{pushPullMap}\mathcal{F}\,\overline{\operatorname{inr}}\bigr) :
8070	    \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,(\operatorname{Over.mk} q) \longrightarrow
8071	    \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_0 \times \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_1,
8072	  \]
8073	  the product-lift of the two push--pull maps of the inclusions (Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}).
8074	  This framing is mandatory, not cosmetic: the downstream section-identification and {\v C}ech-%
8075	  contractibility steps require the forward map of this isomorphism to be \emph{literally} this
8076	  \(\operatorname{prod.lift}\) of push--pull maps; a non-canonical chain isomorphism with the same
8077	  source and target would not serve them. So the task is precisely to prove this canonical map is an
8078	  isomorphism.
8079	
8080	  \emph{The reference chain.} Compare the canonical map against a manifestly invertible composite
8081	  built from the binary disjoint-union decomposition. Write \(P = \operatorname{inl}_*
8082	  (M|_{\operatorname{inl}})\) and \(Q = \operatorname{inr}_*(M|_{\operatorname{inr}})\) for the two
8083	  restriction-to-component pushforwards, and let \(\operatorname{coprodDecompMap} M : M \to P \times
8084	  Q\) be their product-lift of restriction-adjunction units. Then
8085	  \[
8086	    (\operatorname{pushforward} q).\mathrm{obj}\, M
8087	    \xrightarrow{(\operatorname{pushforward} q)(\operatorname{coprodDecompMap} M)}
8088	    (\operatorname{pushforward} q).\mathrm{obj}(P \times Q)
8089	    \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
8090	    (\operatorname{pushforward} q).\mathrm{obj}\, P \times (\operatorname{pushforward} q).\mathrm{obj}\, Q
8091	    \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
8092	    \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_0 \times \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_1
8093	  \]
8094	  is a chain of isomorphisms: the first arrow is \(q_*\) applied to the decomposition iso (an
8095	  isomorphism by the leaf below); the second is that \(q_* = \operatorname{pushforward} q\) preserves
8096	  the binary product \(P \times Q\); the third is \(\operatorname{prod.mapIso}\,\mathrm{idiso}_0\,
8097	  \mathrm{idiso}_1\), the product of the two per-leg identifications \(\mathrm{idiso}_0 :
8098	  (\operatorname{pushforward} q).\mathrm{obj}\, P \cong \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_0\)
8099	  and its \(\operatorname{inr}\)-twin. Each \(\mathrm{idiso}_0\) is assembled by pushing forward along
8100	  \(q_*\) the inner isomorphism that chains the identification of restriction with open-immersion
8101	  pullback (Lemma~\ref{lem:restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}), the pullback-composition comparison
8102	  for \(\operatorname{inl} \circ q\), and the over-triangle reindexing \(q \circ \operatorname{inl} =
8103	  Y_0.\mathrm{hom}\), together with a transport of the codomain along this same triangle. That
8104	  codomain transport is almost entirely definitional: pushforward-composition is identity-on-objects,
8105	  so \(q_*(\operatorname{inl}_* N) = (\operatorname{inl} \circ q)_* N\) holds by definition
8106	  and only the over-triangle relabelling remains.
8107	
8108	  \emph{The leaf — binary disjoint-union decomposition.} The decomposition comparison
8109	  \(\operatorname{coprodDecompMap} M\) is an isomorphism. By
8110	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf} it suffices to check this on sections over each open
8111	  \(V \subseteq Y_0.\mathrm{left} \amalg Y_1.\mathrm{left}\). There the open splits as the disjoint
8112	  union of its two component traces \(\operatorname{inl}^{-1}V\) and \(\operatorname{inr}^{-1}V\)
8113	  (disjoint because the coproduct summands are), so the binary disjoint-union sheaf decomposition ---
8114	  sections over a binary coproduct scheme are the product of the sections over the two preimages,
8115	  Lemma~\ref{lem:coprodPresheafObjIso_mathlib}, equivalently \(F(U \sqcup V') \cong F(U) \times
8116	  F(V')\) for disjoint opens, Lemma~\ref{lem:isProductOfDisjoint_mathlib} --- exhibits \(\Gamma(V, M)
8117	  \cong \Gamma(\operatorname{inl}^{-1}V, M|_{\operatorname{inl}}) \times
8118	  \Gamma(\operatorname{inr}^{-1}V, M|_{\operatorname{inr}})\), and this identification is exactly the
8119	  pair of restriction-adjunction units making up \(\operatorname{coprodDecompMap} M\). (Evaluation at
8120	  \(V\) preserves the binary product, Lemma~\ref{lem:evaluation_preserves_products_mathlib}, so the
8121	  right-hand sections are the corresponding product.) Hence \(\operatorname{coprodDecompMap} M\) is an
8122	  isomorphism, and the reference chain above is an isomorphism.
8123	
8124	  \emph{Matching the canonical map to the chain.} It remains to see the canonical
8125	  \(\operatorname{prod.lift}\) of push--pull maps coincides with the (invertible) reference composite,
8126	  whence it too is an isomorphism. Two maps into a binary product agree once they agree after
8127	  composing with each projection, so it suffices to match the two legs. The
8128	  \(\operatorname{inl}\)-leg of the reference composite is \((\operatorname{pushforward} q).\mathrm{map}
8129	  \,u_0\) followed by \(\mathrm{idiso}_0\), where \(u_0\) is the \(\operatorname{inl}\)-component of
8130	  \(\operatorname{coprodDecompMap} M\); the per-leg coherence identity
8131	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushPull_binary_leg_coherence}) says exactly that this equals
8132	  \(\operatorname{pushPullMap}\mathcal{F}\,\overline{\operatorname{inl}}\), the \(\operatorname{inl}\)-leg
8133	  of the canonical map, and symmetrically for \(\operatorname{inr}\). Therefore the canonical map
8134	  equals the reference isomorphism and is itself an isomorphism in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\).
8135	\end{proof}

8194	\begin{lemma}
8195	[Push--pull on a finite coproduct of legs is the product of the leg push--pulls]
8196	  \label{lem:pushPull_coprod_prod}
8197	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_coprod_prod}
8198	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf, lem:pushPull_binary_coprod_prod}
8199	  Let \((\mathrm{legs} : \iota \to \operatorname{Over} X)\) be a finite family of objects of
8200	  the slice category over a scheme \(X\) (\(\iota\) finite), and form the coproduct
8201	  \(\coprod_{i \in \iota} \mathrm{legs}\,i\) with structure map
8202	  \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}(i \mapsto (\mathrm{legs}\,i).\mathrm{hom}) : \coprod_i
8203	  \mathrm{legs}\,i \to X\). Then the push--pull object on this coproduct is the product of
8204	  the per-leg push--pull objects:
8205	  \[
8206	    \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,\Bigl(\operatorname{Over.mk}\bigl(
8207	      \operatorname{Sigma.desc}(i \mapsto (\mathrm{legs}\,i).\mathrm{hom})\bigr)\Bigr)
8208	      \;\cong\;
8209	    \prod_{i \in \iota} \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,(\mathrm{legs}\,i)
8210	    \qquad \text{in } X.\mathrm{Modules}.
8211	  \]
8212	  This is the general indexed-coproduct\(\to\)product disjoint-union decomposition of a
8213	  module sheaf: the legs may have overlapping images in \(X\), but they are disjoint inside
8214	  the coproduct space \(\coprod_i \mathrm{legs}\,i\), so push--pull splits componentwise.
8215	\end{lemma}
8216	\begin{proof}
8217	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf, lem:pushPull_binary_coprod_prod}
8218	  Argue by induction on the finite index type \(\iota\), iterating the binary decomposition.
8219	
8220	  \emph{Empty base.} If \(\iota\) is empty, \(\coprod_i \mathrm{legs}\,i\) is the initial scheme;

10153	    lem:pushforward_iso_qcoh_of_slice_qcoh, lem:presentation_map_mathlib,
10154	    lem:presentation_ofIsIso_mathlib, lem:nonempty_quasicoherentData_mathlib,
10155	    lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}
10156	  Quasi-coherence is a \emph{local} datum: it transports across the equivalence \(\Phi\) by moving
10157	  \(H\)'s local presentations to the image cover, member by member. We never seek a global presentation
10158	  of \(H\) (it has none — \(H\) is quasi-coherent only on the abstract source); we move the local
10159	  presentations one open at a time. The carrying functor need not be the full
10160	  restricted-module equivalence: a single \emph{left adjoint} pullback functor along one cross-ring
10161	  slice structure-sheaf ring map suffices, which keeps the colimit-preservation that
10162	  presentation-transport requires while collapsing the heavy equivalence bookkeeping to one ring map,
10163	  one unit isomorphism, and one comparison isomorphism.
10164	
10165	  \emph{The cover.} Extract \(H\)'s quasi-coherence datum
10166	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:nonempty_quasicoherentData_mathlib}): a family \((U_i)_{i \in I}\) of opens covering
10167	  \(X\) together with, for each \(i\), a presentation \(p_i\) of \(H.\operatorname{over} U_i\). Form the
10168	  image cover \(V_i = \varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}\,U_i\) of \(Y\) (the image of an affine open under the
10169	  scheme isomorphism is again affine, Lemma~\ref{lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}, when one wants
10170	  the cover to remain affine). By Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_iso_qcoh_of_slice_qcoh} it suffices to show
10171	  that \((\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H).\operatorname{over} V_i\) is quasi-coherent for every
10172	  \(i\).
10173

10175	  \varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}\,W\). Let \(\psi_r\) be the slice structure-sheaf ring map of
10176	  Lemma~\ref{lem:slice_structureSheaf_hom}, and consider the pullback functor
10177	  \(\operatorname{pullback}\psi_r\). Being a left adjoint, it preserves colimits, so by
10178	  Lemma~\ref{lem:presentation_map_mathlib} the presentation \(p_i\) of \(H.\operatorname{over} W\)
10179	  (transported across the opens-equivalence) transports to a presentation of
10180	  \((\operatorname{pullback}\psi_r).\mathrm{obj}(H.\operatorname{over} W \text{ transported})\). The
10181	  comparison isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_slice_pullback_iso},
10182	  \[
10183	    \bigl(\operatorname{pullback}\psi_r\bigr).\mathrm{obj}\bigl(H.\operatorname{over} W
10184	      \text{ transported}\bigr)
10185	      \;\cong\;
10186	    \bigl(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H\bigr).\operatorname{over} V,
10187	  \]
10188	  then carries that presentation, via Lemma~\ref{lem:presentation_ofIsIso_mathlib}, to a presentation of
10189	  \((\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H).\operatorname{over} V\). A sheaf of modules with a (global)
10190	  presentation is quasi-coherent, so each member of the image cover is quasi-coherent, and the claim
10191	  follows by Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_iso_qcoh_of_slice_qcoh}.
10192	
10193	  This is the isomorphism case of the Stacks functoriality remark (``it is in general not the case that
10194	  the pushforward of a quasi-coherent module is quasi-coherent''; here it holds because \(\varphi\) is an
10195	  isomorphism, equivalently \(\varphi_* = (\varphi^{-1})^*\) is a pullback).
10196	\end{proof}
